html:
Svante August Arrhenius, born on February 19, 1859, at Viks estate in the parish of Balingsta (Uppsala County), and died on October 2, 1927, in Stockholm, was a Swedish physicist and chemist who worked in many areas of natural science. He was a professor of physics at the University of Stockholm from 1895 to 1905, and later served as rector of the university from 1897 to 1902. He became the first Swedish Nobel laureate when he was awarded the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 1903.

In his work from 1896 on the impact of carbon dioxide on soil temperature, he described how the Earth would slowly warm up, and thus became a pioneer in understanding the greenhouse effect.

Svante Arrhenius is often considered, along with Wilhelm Ostwald and Jacobus Henricus van't Hoff, as one of the founders of physical chemistry.

== Early Life and Education == Svante Arrhenius was the son of Svante Gustaf Arrhenius (1813-1885) and Carolina Christina Thunberg (1820-1906), and the nephew of Johan Arrhenius. Arrhenius's date of birth is given in various sources as either February 18 or 19, 1859. The birth record, some household registers, and both editions of the Encyclopedia Britannica state his birthday as February 18, while other household registers, the Nobel Committee's biography, and the Royal Swedish Academy of Engineering Sciences' memorial publication indicate his date of birth as February 19 of the same year. Arrhenius himself seems to have celebrated his birthday on February 19. His ancestors were farmers, and his father had studied surveying and graduated in 1834. The father was employed by Uppsala University from 1847 as the administrator of the university's estates in western Uppland. After marrying in 1855, he took on a second position as the administrator of Viks estate, where Svante Arrhenius was born as the family's second child. The family moved to Uppsala in 1860, where his father became an academic official.

Svante Arrhenius attended Katedralskolan (Cathedral School) and showed an aptitude for mathematics at a young age, also developing an interest in physics. In 1876, he passed the university entrance examination in Uppsala with good grades, although not as the top student in his class.

After graduating from high school, he began studying mathematics, chemistry, and physics at Uppsala University, along with history, mineralogy, geology, botany, and Latin. In January 1878, he became a candidate for a degree (filosofie kandidat), completing it in just one and a half years, which was a record-breaking achievement. After a trip to Paris during the summer, he began his doctoral studies in the fall of 1878. Initially, this involved lectures in mathematics and laboratory work in chemistry. After this introduction, Arrhenius focused on choosing physics as his main subject but wanted to study the area at the intersection of physics and chemistry. The professor of physics at Uppsala considered this inappropriate and suggested that Arrhenius should choose chemistry as his main subject. Therefore, Arrhenius, together with his friend Claes Mebius, contacted Erik Edlund, a physicist at the Royal Swedish Academy of Sciences in Stockholm, to explore the possibility of having him as an advisor instead. These two, along with doctoral student Lukas Homén, helped Edlund with his research on electrical discharges. During this period, Arrhenius remained active in student life in Uppsala, particularly at Uplands Nation.

In the spring of 1882, Edlund offered that the three could continue working in his laboratory, but with research areas of their own choosing. Arrhenius chose to study a method for determining the molecular weight of chemical compounds in solution, and later that year, he began his studies of the conductivity of electrolytes, which became his first major research endeavor and the subject of his doctoral dissertation. In June 1883, a first draft of his doctoral dissertation was completed, but it did not impress his chemistry professor at Uppsala University. However, Otto Pettersson, the chemistry professor at the newly established Stockholm University College, was impressed with Arrhenius's results and suggested some changes to the dissertation. Arrhenius partially revised his dissertation based on Pettersson's suggestions.

In 1884, Arrhenius became a licentiate and doctor of philosophy at Uppsala University, after defending his dissertation on May 26, 1884. He was approved during the defense, but received an average grade on the dissertation.

== Academic Career ==

Because his doctoral dissertation was considered substandard in Uppsala, he was not appointed as a lecturer (docent) after its defense. This made it very difficult for him to pursue an academic career. Arrhenius sent his dissertation to the two leading early physical chemists, Jacobus Henricus van 't Hoff in Amsterdam and Wilhelm Ostwald in Riga, hoping to interest them in his findings. After some correspondence, Ostwald traveled to Uppsala to meet with him and offered him a lecturer position in Riga, which Arrhenius declined (his father was seriously ill at the time and passed away in 1885). Ostwald's interest helped demonstrate that Arrhenius' dissertation was actually of very high quality and contained groundbreaking results. As a result, he was appointed as a lecturer in physical chemistry at Uppsala University (in an unpaid position) at the end of 1884.

Thanks to a three-year travel grant of 4,500 Swedish krona from the Royal...

How certain are we about this?

Information about people from the 20th century is extremely well-documented. Uncertainty rarely concerns facts themselves, but rather interpretations and evaluations, which can change over time and depending on the observer.
tldr: Svante Arrhenius was a Swedish scientist who became the first Swede to receive the Nobel Prize in Chemistry. He is known for discovering the greenhouse effect and explaining how carbon dioxide affects the Earth's temperature.
lattlast:
Svante Arrhenius was born in 1859 and studied physics and chemistry at Uppsala University. He was a highly gifted student who quickly completed his studies and later became a professor and rector at the Stockholm University College. In 1903, he received the Nobel Prize in Chemistry, making him the first Swede to win the prize.

Arrhenius is best known for his work on the greenhouse effect, which he described as early as 1896. He calculated how carbon dioxide in the atmosphere causes a gradual increase in Earth's temperature. For this reason, he is considered a pioneer in climate research.

In addition to his work on climate, Arrhenius was an important figure in physical chemistry, a field that studies chemical substances using the laws of physics. Along with two other researchers, he is considered one of the founders of this scientific discipline.
